examples.title: Checking Universal Quantifier Truth Value
examples.description: Algorithm to evaluate ∀x P(x) under a given interpretation
examples.code:
FUNCTION CheckUniversalQuantifier(domain D, predicate P):
 FOR EACH element e IN D:
 IF NOT P(e) THEN
 RETURN FALSE // Counterexample found
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN TRUE // All elements satisfy P
examples.explanation: Universal quantifier ∀x P(x) is TRUE if and only if predicate P holds for EVERY element in the domain. If even one element fails, the universal statement is FALSE.

examples.title: Checking Existential Quantifier Truth Value
examples.description: Algorithm to evaluate ∃x P(x) under a given interpretation
examples.code:
FUNCTION CheckExistentialQuantifier(domain D, predicate P):
 FOR EACH element e IN D:
 IF P(e) THEN
 RETURN TRUE // Found satisfying element
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN FALSE // No element satisfies P
examples.explanation: Existential quantifier ∃x P(x) is TRUE if and only if predicate P holds for AT LEAST ONE element in the domain. It is FALSE only when NO element satisfies P.

examples.title: Term Evaluation in Interpretations
examples.description: Algorithm to evaluate a term under interpretation and variable assignment
examples.code:
FUNCTION EvaluateTerm(term t, interpretation I, assignment g):
 IF t is constant c THEN
 RETURN I_constant(c) // Return assigned element
 ELSE IF t is variable v THEN
 RETURN g(v) // Return assigned value
 ELSE IF t is function f(t1, t2, ..., tn) THEN
 args = []
 FOR i = 1 TO n:
 args.append(EvaluateTerm(ti, I, g))
 END FOR
 RETURN I_function(f)(args) // Apply function to evaluated args
 END IF
examples.explanation: Terms evaluate to elements in the domain. Constants map directly to domain elements via interpretation; variables get values from assignment; functions apply the interpreted function to evaluated arguments.

examples.title: Atomic Formula Evaluation
examples.description: Algorithm to evaluate atomic predicate formulas under interpretation
examples.code:
FUNCTION EvaluateAtomic(predicate P, term_list args, interpretation I, assignment g):
 evaluated_args = []
 FOR EACH term t IN args:
 evaluated_args.append(EvaluateTerm(t, I, g))
 END FOR
 RETURN I_predicate(P)(evaluated_args) // True if relation holds
examples.explanation: Atomic formulas evaluate to TRUE or FALSE. Each term is evaluated to get domain elements, then the predicate (relation) is checked on those elements according to the interpretation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of arguments

examples.title: Full Formula Evaluation (Recursive)
examples.description: Complete algorithm to evaluate any FOL formula under interpretation
examples.code:
FUNCTION EvaluateFormula(formula φ, interpretation I, assignment g):
 SWITCH φ:
 CASE atomic P(t1,...,tn):
 RETURN EvaluateAtomic(P, [t1,...,tn], I, g)
 
 CASE ¬ψ:
 RETURN NOT EvaluateFormula(ψ, I, g)
 
 CASE ψ1 ∧ ψ2:
 RETURN EvaluateFormula(ψ1, I, g) AND EvaluateFormula(ψ2, I, g)
 
 CASE ψ1 ∨ ψ2:
 RETURN EvaluateFormula(ψ1, I, g) OR EvaluateFormula(ψ2, I, g)
 
 CASE ψ1 → ψ2:
 RETURN (NOT EvaluateFormula(ψ1, I, g)) OR EvaluateFormula(ψ2, I, g)
 
 CASE ∀x ψ:
 FOR EACH element e IN I.domain:
 g_new = g with x assigned to e
 IF NOT EvaluateFormula(ψ, I, g_new) THEN
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN TRUE
 
 CASE ∃x ψ:
 FOR EACH element e IN I.domain:
 g_new = g with x assigned to e
 IF EvaluateFormula(ψ, I, g_new) THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN FALSE
examples.explanation: This recursive evaluator handles all FOL connectives and quantifiers. For quantified formulas, it iterates through all domain elements with appropriate variable assignments. This is the foundation of semantic evaluation in FOL.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n) worst case for n quantified variables
